content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 12, 1988

Richard E. Archibald
Deputy City Attorney
City of Sacramento
812 Tenth Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-88-259

Dear Mr. Archibald:

     We have received your letter concerning the new restrictions on publicly-funded mass mailings under Proposition 73.

     Enclosed is a copy of follow-up advice to Vance Raye and other state and local agency representatives interpreting the mass mailing restrictions. This letter and the June 16, 1988 letter are intended as guidance to all persons similarly situated, and may therefore be relied upon until the Commission takes further action.

     In regard to your specific question concerning notices of "town hall meetings" sent at public expense, please refer to paragraph 3 on page 4 of the July 1, 1988 letter. Based on that paragraph, your Exhibit "2" would be permitted because it contains no reference to any elected official. However, your Exhibit "3" could not be prepared and sent at public expense because it bears the signature of an elected official.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Richard E. Archibald
July 12, 1988
Page 2


     In the future, we expect to address additional questions
concerning the mass mailing restrictions in Proposition 73, and
we will include your name on our mailing list for information
on the subject.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

                                        [illegible]
                                        By: Kathryn E. Donovan
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

DMG:KED:ld

Enclosure

OFFICE OF THE
CITY ATTORNEY

CITY OF SACRAMENTO
CALIFORNIA

812 TENTH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CA
95814-2694

JAMES P. JACKSON
CITY ATTORNEY

June 28, 1988

916-449-5346

THEODORE H. KOBEY, JR.
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

DEPUTY CITY
ATTORNEYS:
SAMUEL L. JACKSON
WILLIAM P. CARNAZZO
LAWRENCE F. M. LANDEN
GARLAND E. BURRELL, JR.
DIANE B. BALTIER
RICHARD F. ANTOINE
TAMARA MILLIGANHARMON
RICHARD E. ARCHIBALD
KATHLEEN L. McCORMICK

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Mass Mailing Provisions
of Proposition 73

Dear Ms. Griffiths:

     This office has received numerous requests from City officials
and staff concerning the meaning and effect of the "mass mailings"
provisions of Proposition 73. While I have discussed some of
these matters with representatives of your office over the tele-
phone, I would like to request a formal written opinion on some of
these questions pursuant to Government Code Section 83114.

     This office has received and reviewed your June 16, 1988
letter directed to Vance Raye and other legal representatives of
various state and local governmental agencies and entities. While
this letter addresses some of the questions presented to this
office, there are other questions which remain unanswered. The
purpose of this letter is to request an opinion as to some of the
unanswered questions, and to confirm that the City and its offi-
cials, agents and employees may rely upon the advice and opinions
set forth in your June 16, 1988 letter.

     Pursuant to Government Code Section 83114, this office re-
spectfully requests on behalf of the City of Sacramento, and its
elected and appointed officials, agents and employees, an opinion
addressing the following questions:

     1. May the City of Sacramento, and its elected and appointed
officials, agents, and employees, rely upon the advice contained
in your June 16, 1988 letter to Vance Raye et al. (copy attached
as Exhibit "1" hereto)?

Diana M. Griffiths, General Counsel
California Fair Political Practices Comm.
June 28, 1988
Page Two


2. In the past, the City of Sacramento has held "town hall
meetings" at various locations throughout the City for the purpose
of discussing matters of general concern or, in some instances,
particular events, projects and issues. These meetings are not
mandatory pursuant to any federal, state or local law, and are
held for the purpose of disseminating or gathering information or
opinions about various issues or topics of concern. Elected officials may or may not be present at these "town hall meetings".

(a) May the City send, at public expense, notices of these
"town hall meetings" to 200 or more people if such notices do
not identify the elected official(s) who will be present? A
draft of a proposed notice of a "town hall meeting" is
attached as Exhibit "2" hereto.

(b) Assuming that the notice referenced in (a), above, is
permissible, may the City send, at public expense, notices of
the "town hall meetings" to 200 or more people if such notices
will identify the particular official(s) to be present? A
draft of a proposed notice is attached as Exhibit "3" hereto.

Your prompt response to the foregoing questions would be
greatly appreciated. If you have any questions, or further explanation is needed, please do not hesitate to contact the undersigned.

Very truly yours,

JAMES P. JACKSON, City Attorney

By: [signature]
RICHARD E. ARCHIBALD,
Deputy City Attorney

REA/jmv
Attachments

sections.question_synthetic: Whether town hall meeting notices sent at public expense must comply with Proposition 73's mass mailing restrictions, specifically whether the presence of an elected official's signature affects permissibility.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Exhibit 2 (town hall notice with no reference to elected official) may be sent at public expense, but Exhibit 3 (bearing an elected official's signature) cannot be prepared and sent at public expense under Proposition 73 restrictions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document structured as advice letter response rather than traditional Q/A format. Question and conclusion are inferred from body text rather than explicitly labeled sections. References prior FPPC letter dated July 1, 1988 for detailed guidance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether town hall meeting notices sent at public expense must comply with Proposition 73's mass mailing restrictions, specifically whether the presence of an elected official's signature affects permissibility.

CONCLUSION: Exhibit 2 (town hall notice with no reference to elected official) may be sent at public expense, but Exhibit 3 (bearing an elected official's signature) cannot be prepared and sent at public expense under Proposition 73 restrictions.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the City of Sacramento's inquiry about whether town hall meeting notices sent at public expense comply with Proposition 73's mass mailing restrictions. The FPPC advises that notices without elected official references are permitted, but those bearing an elected official's signature are prohibited.
